---
title: "Lab Assignment #1"
author: "Math 437 - Modern Data Analysis - Jason Soto"
format: html
editor: source
---

## Instructions

This is a Quarto file. This file allows you to include both your code and your commentary in a single file. All lab work - code and answers - should be done in this notebook.

This lab assignment is worth a total of **17.5 points**.

## Quarto

Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished document. To learn more about Quarto see <https://quarto.org>.

## Running Code

When you click the **Render** button a document will be generated that includes both content and the output of embedded code. You can embed code like this:

```{r}
1 + 1
```

You can add options to executable code like this

```{r}
#| echo: false
2 * 2
```

The `echo: false` option disables the printing of code (only output is displayed).

## Additional Help from Dr. Wynne

When you click the **Render** button, R Studio will create (or overwrite) an HTML file containing your answers as well as the code and output. Note that creating a pdf file directly requires your $LaTeX$ distribution to work nicely with R Studio. If you get things to work, you can create a pdf file directly by changing the `format: html` line to `format: pdf`. Note that you may have to do some of fiddling with this source file to get everything to show up correctly in the pdf file, so if you go that route, don't change the output type until you have your solutions totally ready to go.

Code in Quarto documents is written in chunks. If you are using the *Visual* editor, add a new chunk by clicking *Insert* then *Executable Cell* and choosing the language you will be writing the code in (you probably want R for now). In the *Source* editor, which I highly recommend using once you have the basics down, add a chunk by pressing *Ctrl/Cmd+Alt+I*. 

I recommend organizing your chunks to look like:

```{r}
#| label: name your chunk here
#| eval: FALSE
# write your R comments here
write your R code here
```

I recommend starting each chunk with a unique chunk name that tells you what you are doing in that chunk (when you get error messages, you want to know which chunk contains the error instead of guessing what `unnamed-chunk-30` corresponds to). On the first line of the chunk, type `#| label: ` followed by the name you want to give the chunk.

You can follow the `#| label: ` line with any other options for how R Studio should print the code and output. Each option should go on its own line and be proceeded by `#|`. You don't always need to do this, but you occasionally will need to use these common options:

-   `echo`: As we saw in the default instructions, `echo: false` hides the code in the chunk.
-   `eval`: by default `eval: true` means R Studio will run the code chunk and display the output when you render the document. If your chunk produces an error, set `eval: false`. Some of the chunks in the original lab and homework files set `eval: FALSE` by default so that I can make sure the original file renders; please make sure to delete this option once you get the chunk to run.
-   `include`: by default R Studio will display the code (if `echo: true`) and results (if `eval: true`) for the chunk. Use `include: false` if you want R Studio to run the code but display neither the code nor the output.
-   `message`: by default R Studio will print in the file any messages from R. You can use `message: false` if there is an annoying persistent message that isn't informative. I tend to do this in the code chunks that I load packages.
-   `warning`: by default R Studio will print any warning messages generated by the code (the code still runs though). You can use `warning: false` if you don't want, for example, a bunch of reminders about the version of R the most updated versions of the packages were written in.

To run an individual chunk, click the green triangle (Play button) in the top right of the chunk. The downward-facing arrow to the left of the Play button indicates to run all previous chunks in the document.

## Problem 1: Introduction to R (Code: 1 pt)

All you need to do for this problem is to practice running each chunk. You may want to make notes to yourself as you run these chunks.

At the beginning of each lab file, I include a chunk that imports the packages (a package is a set of additional functions and data) and datasets you will be using. When you create your own files, you should follow this convention.

For example, let's load the `ISLR2` package. If you do not have this package installed, you should see a message at the top of the Quarto document window telling you this, and you can click *Install* to fix the issue directly. You can also click on the *Packages* tab in RStudio, click *Install*, and type the name of the package you want to install.

```{r}
#| label: load ISLR2 package
#| warning: false
library(ISLR2)  # load the library
```

If the dataset we are using is already in an installed package, you can simply load the data directly. For example, let's look at the *Auto* dataset in the ISLR2 package.

```{r}
#| label: load Auto data
View(Auto) # View the data in a separate window
head(Auto) # View the first few rows as output
```

The first thing you want to do after importing a dataset is do some sanity checks to make sure everything imported okay. 

Typically you should check that the number of observations and number of variables in the dataset are what you expected:

```{r}
#| label: check dimensions
dim(Auto)  # number of observations and number of variables
```

It's also good to check the names of your variables. Generally, variable names in R should be one word or multiple words separated by underscores (_) or periods (.).

```{r}
#| label: check variable names
names(Auto)  # names of variables in the dataset
colnames(Auto) # usually the same thing 
```

Sometimes it may be useful to see a summary of the variables in the dataset, including missing data.

```{r}
#| label: look at summary
summary(Auto)  # brief summary of each variable in the dataset
```

To view information about a dataset or help about a function, you can type `?` followed by the name of the dataset or function, or just search for it in the *Help* tab in R Studio.

## Problem 2: More Basic R Commands (2 pts total)

### Part a (Code: 1 pt)

ISLR Lab 2.3.1.

#### Solution

Practice adding chunks and typing in the code directly. Every time you see a new chunk in the lab, create a new chunk in the Quarto document and *manually type in* the code in the chunk, then run that code.

While you can technically copy/paste from the pdf, the goal in Problem 2 is to understand how R syntax works, and it's really hard to gain any understanding from just copy/pasting.

You do not need to `label` each chunk, but it may be helpful if you are working with R for the first time.

```{r}
#| label: First Chunk 1
x <- c(1, 3, 2, 5)
x
```

```{r}
#| label: Second Chunk 1
x= c(1, 6, 2)
x
y= c(1, 4, 3)
```

```{r}
#| label: Third Chunk 1
length(x)
length(y)
x+y
```

```{r}
#| label: Fourth Chunk 1
ls()
rm(x, y)
```

```{r}
#| label: Fifth Chunk 1
ls()
```

```{r}
#| label: Sixth Chunk 1
rm(list = ls())
```

```{r}
#| label: Seventh Chunk 1
?matrix
```

```{r}
#| label: Eight Chunk 1
x <- matrix(data = c(1, 2, 3, 4), nrow = 2, ncol = 2)
x

```

```{r}
#| label: Ninth Chunk 1
x <- matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), 2, 2)
```

```{r}
#| label: Tenth Chunk 1
matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), 2, 2, byrow = TRUE)
```

```{r}
#| label: Eleventh Chunk 1
sqrt(x)
x^2
```

```{r}
#| label: Twelfth Chunk
sqrt(x)
x^2
```

```{r}
#| label: Thirteenth Chunk 1
x <- rnorm(50)
y <- x + rnorm(50, mean = 50, sd = .1)
cor(x, y)
```

```{r}
#| label: Fourteenth Chunk 1
set.seed(3)
y <- rnorm(100)
mean(y)
var(y)
sqrt(var(y))
sd(y)
```

### Part b (Explanation: 1 pt)

Explain in your own words the purpose of the `set.seed()` function. What happens if you *don't* include this line before running the `rnorm` function?

#### Solution

In my own words, the purpose of the `set.seed()` function is to be able to reproduce random numbers. In Minecraft terms as this function is used in world production, the world is randomly generated, but with a seed, you can reproduce the same randomly generated world. Hence, numbers are randomly generated, and a seed allows us to access the same randomly selected numbers.
If `set.seed()` is not included before running the `rnorm` function, we will simply achieve random numbers each time.

## Problem 3: Indexing Data (2 pts total)

### Part a (Code: 1 pt)

ISLR Lab 2.3.3. 

### Solution

Just like in Problem 2, every time you see a new chunk of code in the lab, create a new chunk in this file, manually type in the code, and then run it.

```{r}
#| label: First Chunk 2
A <- matrix(1:16, 4, 4)
A
```

```{r}
#| label: Second Chunk 2
A[2, 3]
```

```{r}
#| label: Third Chunk 2
A[c(1, 3), c(2, 4)]
A[1:3, 2:4]
A[1:2, ]
A[, 1:2]
```

```{r}
#| label: Fourth Chunk 2
A[1, ]
```

```{r}
#| label: Fifth Chunk 2
A[-c(1, 3), ]
A[-c(1, 3), -c(1, 3, 4)]
```

```{r}
#| label: Sixth Chunk 2
dim(A)
```

### Part b (Code: 1 pt)

When you import data into R, it usually will be stored in a *data frame* object instead of a matrix. There are a variety of ways to extract an individual column from a data frame.

```{r}
#| label: create Auto2
Auto2 <- Auto[1:10,]  # first 10 rows for convenience
```

```{r}
#| label: subset Auto2 part 1
Auto2[[1]]
```

```{r}
#| label: subset Auto2 part 2
Auto2$mpg
```

Note that each of these methods extracts the column as a *vector*. There are also ways to extract the column as a one-column data frame:

```{r}
#| label: subset Auto2 part 3
Auto2["mpg"]
```

Then there is this way of subsetting:

```{r}
#| label: subset Auto2 part 4
Auto2[, c(1, 2)] # two-column data frame
Auto2[, 1] # not a data frame!?
```

This is the standard and yet most confusing way to subset a data frame. If you include multiple columns, the output will stay a data frame. If you include a single column, you will get the column as a vector, unless one of the following is true:

1. You explicitly tell R not to do that, e.g., `Auto2[, 1, drop = FALSE]`
2. You are working with a special type of data frame called a `tibble`, in which returning another tibble is the default no matter how many columns you choose. Whenever we use `From Text (readr)` to read in our csv files, they will be read in as tibbles, but it is good practice to check.

In the code chunks below, find two different ways to subset the `Auto2` data to get a two-column dataset containing `mpg` and `horsepower` (columns 1 and 4). 

#### Solution

```{r}
#| label: subset using numbers
Auto2[, c(1, 4)]
```


```{r}
#| label: subset using variable names
Auto2[, c("mpg", "horsepower")]
```

## Problem 4: Graphing with Base R (5 pts total)

For parts (a) through (c):

* Create the requested plot using the *Auto* dataset. Make sure each plot has appropriate labels for the x-and y-axis. You can find all of the relevant code in ISLR Lab 2.3.5.
* Write a short paragraph describing the graph to someone who does not know anything about the *Auto* dataset. When I describe a graph, I use the TAME strategy to orient the reader/listener:

* **T**opic: what data (observational units and variables) you are graphing
* **A**xes: what variables are being mapped to the x-axis and y-axis (and other properties, e.g., color)
* **M**ain point: the most important takeaway (for one variable, this usually deals with the mode of the distribution; for two variables, this usually deals with the association)
* **E**xtra information (optional): anything else that you find interesting to point out


### Part a (Code: 0.5 pts; Explanation: 1 pt)

Create and describe a scatterplot of `mpg` (response) against `cylinders` (numerical predictor). Do not attach the dataset; use the `$` sign to select the variables.

#### Solution

The graph below is a scatter plot linking mpg and amount of cylinders in cars, with mpg on the y-axis and cylinders on the x-axis. The purpose of this graph is to see the effect that cylinder amounts have on mpg. There appears to be correlation, a negative effect.

```{r}
#| label: scatterplot of mpg against cylinders
plot(Auto$cylinders, Auto$mpg)
attach(Auto)
plot(cylinders, mpg)
```


### Part b (Code: 0.5 pts; Explanation: 1 pt)

Create and describe a boxplot of `mpg` (response) by `cylinders` (categorical predictor). The boxes should be horizontal and red.

#### Solution

```{r}
#| label: boxplot of mpg by number of cylinders
cylinders <- as.factor(cylinders)
plot(cylinders, mpg)                                                                  # plain
plot(cylinders , mpg, col = "red")                                                    # gameboy color type shit
plot(cylinders , mpg, col = "red", varwidth = T)                                      # variable width adds proportional width to variables
plot(cylinders , mpg, col = "red", varwidth = T, horizontal = T)                      #horizontal
plot(cylinders , mpg, col = "red", varwidth = T, xlab = "cylinders", ylab = "MPG")    # definitive

```

### Part c (Code: 0.5 pts; Explanation: 1 pt)

Create and describe a histogram of `mpg`. The bars should be red and there should be roughly 15 bars.

#### Solution

```{r}
#| label: histogram of mpg
hist(mpg)                           # plain
hist(mpg, col = 2)                  # gbc
hist(mpg, col = 2, breaks = 15)     # definitive, bars added
```

### Part d (Code: 0.5 pts)

Using the `pairs` function, create a scatterplot matrix showing `mpg`, `displacement`, `horsepower`, `weight`, and `acceleration`.

#### Solution

```{r}
#| label: scatterplot matrix
pairs(Auto)                                                                  # creates scatterplot matrix
pairs(~ mpg + displacement + horsepower + weight + acceleration, data = Auto) # custom scatterplot 
```

## Problem 5: Introduction to Functions (5 pts total)

### Part a (Code: 0.5 pts)

In the chunk below, write a single line of R code that prints the result of raising the number 2 to the 3rd power. Explicitly use the `print` function.

```{r}
#| label: two to the third
print(2^3)
```

### Part b (Code and Testing: 1 pt)

Complete the R chunk below to create and test a function that prints the result of raising *any* number to the 3rd power. Make sure to delete the "eval: FALSE" line in the chunk options after you get a chunk that works the way you expect!

```{r}
#| label: cube function
Cube <- function(x){print(x^3)}
```

It is always a good idea to test any function you write to make sure that it works as you expected.

What do you expect to get when you call the `Cube` function on a single number? What do you expect to get when you input a vector of numbers instead of a single number? What do you expect to get when you input a character string instead of a number? 

Make guesses, then run the chunks in the Solution section to verify whether your guesses were correct.

#### Solution

For each chunk below, if you get an output, remove `eval = FALSE` from the corresponding chunk options; if you get an error, keep `eval = FALSE` so that you can still knit without running the code.

```{r}
#| label: test cube function with single number
Cube(x = 2)
Cube(2)
```

```{r}
#| label: test cube function with vector
Cube(c(2, 5, 10))
```

```{r}
#| label: test cube function with character
#| eval: false
# remove the eval: false line if you get this code to work!
# otherwise keep it so you can render the document
Cube("Math 437")
```

### Part c (Code and Testing: 0.5 pts)

Now, we will write another function that prints the result of raising *any* number to *any* exponent. This requires two arguments to the function: the base and the exponent.

It is good form to document all arguments to your function, either before the function or in the first few lines.

```{r}
#| label: power function
Power <- function(x, a){print(x^a)}
```

For each of the three lines of code in the chunk below, make a prediction about what you will get out, then run the chunk and determine whether you got what you expected.

#### Solution

```{r}
#| label: test power function
Power(x = 2, a = 3)
Power(a = 2, x = 3)
Power(2, 3)
```


### Part d (Code and Testing: 0.5 pts)

What do you expect to get when you use a vector instead of a number for the first argument? What about for the second argument? What about for both arguments?

Make your predictions, then run the chunk below and determine if your predictions were correct.

#### Solution

```{r}
#| label: test power function with vectors
Power(c(2, 5, 10), 3)
Power(2, c(2, 3, 4))
Power(c(2, 5, 10), c(1, 2, 3))
```

### Part e (Code and Testing: 0.5 pts)

What do you expect to get if you only pass in one argument to the `Power` function? Make a prediction, then run the chunk below to see whether your prediction was correct.

#### Solution

```{r}
#| label: test power function with one argument
#| eval: false
# remove the eval: false line if you get this code to work!
# otherwise keep it so you can render the document
Power(2)
```

### Part f (Code and Testing: 0.5 pts)

Usually we want to *return* the result of the function rather than *print* it, so that we can store the output in an R object.

```{r}
#| label: Power_return function
Power_return <- function(x, a){return(x^a)}
```

Let's confirm that we still get $2^3 = 8$ out when we run this line of code.

```{r}
#| label: test Power_return function
Power_return(2, 3)
```

### Part g (Explanation: 1 pt)

Explain the difference between these two lines of code. (Remember to remove `eval = FALSE` after you get the chunk to run the way it's supposed to.) Where did the 8 go when you ran the second line?

```{r}
#| label: test Power_return function with assignment
result1 <- Power(2, 3)
result2 <- Power_return(2, 3)
```

#### Solution

The 8 is sent straight to "result2" as it was not ordered to print, right?

### Part h (Code and Testing: 0.5 pts)

What if we *usually* want to cube a number, but we also want to give the user the flexibility to include a different exponent if necessary? We can use a *default* argument:

```{r}
#| label: Power_default function
Power_default <- function(x, a = 3){return(x^a)}
```

Let's confirm that we still get $2^3 = 8$ out when we run this line of code.

```{r}
#| label: test Power_default function
Power_default(2, 3)
```

What do you expect to get if you only pass in one argument to `Power_default`? Make a prediction, then run the chunk below to see whether your prediction was correct. 

#### Solution

```{r}
#| label: test Power_default with one argument
Power_default(2)
```

By convention, put arguments without a default value at the beginning of the list of arguments and arguments with a default value at the end.

## Problem 6: More Work with Functions (2.5 pts total)

In this problem, we are going to write a slightly more complex function to standardize a vector of data (convert to estimated z-scores).

### Part a (Code: 1 pt)

Consider the steps necessary to standardize a vector of data:

* Compute the mean and standard deviation of the data
* Subtract the mean from each data value, and divide the result by the standard deviation

Using the commands learned so far in this lab, complete the chunk below.

#### Solution

```{r}
my_vector <- c(1, 4, 6, 7, 9, 10, 12, 15)

xbar <- mean(my_vector)
s <- sd(my_vector)

z <- (my_vector-xbar)/s

print(z)
```

### Part b (Code: 1 pt)

Now we are ready to turn these lines of code into a function. Follow the commented instructions in the chunk below to create the `z_score` function.

#### Solution

```{r}
#| label: z-score function
z_score <- function(x){
xbar <- mean(x)
s <- sd(x)
z <- (x-xbar)/s
return(z)                    # instead of print
}
```


### Part c (Testing: 0.5 pts)

Now let's test the function.

```{r}
#| label: compute z-score with z_score function
my_vector2 <- c(3, 6, 18, 1)
z_score(my_vector2)
```

If you did everything correctly, then R will output the correct z-scores of approximately -0.525, -0.131, 1.444, and -0.788. If you didn't get those numbers, make sure that you replaced all instances of `my_vector` inside your function with `x`.

Even though we didn't pass `my_vector` to the function, it's still hanging out in the Global Environment (see the *Environment* panel). Since there's no `my_vector` inside the function, R will guess that it's supposed to use the `my_vector` in the Global Environment.

Environments are a weird and advanced topic, and I point this issue out specifically because it's the number two cause of Rendering issues (behind code errors). When you run the chunks, R looks for variables in the Global Environment. But when you Render the document, R creates an entirely new Environment that doesn't have access to anything in the Global Environment. If everything works when you run the code chunks individually but you get errors when you Render the document, it's very likely that there's a variable hanging out in your Global Environment that isn't defined in the document. Click the broom icon in the *Environment* panel to delete everything from your Global Environment, then rerun your code chunks to see which variable(s) you forgot to define in the document.
